summary: 11 concrete items surfaced from 24 qualifying stories; 24/24 stories scraped; history updated.

items.name: Qwen3.6-27B
items.source_story_title: Qwen3.6-27B: Flagship-Level Coding in a 27B Dense Model
items.summary: A 27B local coding model commenters found unusually strong in practice.
items.why_included: It drew repeated praise as a serious local alternative for coding and audits.
items.evidence: Commenters called the quantized build excellent, shared good security-audit results, and even said it beat an Opus 4.7 run for them.

items.name: Broccoli
items.source_story_title: Show HN: Broccoli, one shot coding agent on the cloud
items.summary: An open-source cloud harness that runs coding tasks in isolated sandboxes.
items.why_included: It solves the agent-harness problem instead of just shipping another model wrapper.
items.evidence: The thread emphasized Linear tasks, sandboxed execution, and claims that non-developer PRs shipped through Broccoli.

items.name: Zed
items.summary: A fast editor adding parallel agents and worktree-based AI workflows.
items.why_included: It marries a beloved editor with a concrete multi-agent workflow.
items.evidence: Commenters called Zed one of the best editors in years and debated the new parallel-agent layout.

items.name: flipbook.page
items.source_story_title: Website streamed live directly from a model
items.summary: A live model-built website demo that commenters found unusually original.
items.why_included: It stood out as a novel AI demo with clear educational potential.
items.evidence: HN called it one of the more unique ideas and an amazing educational tool despite speed and hallucination issues.

items.name: Ursa Ag
items.source_story_title: Alberta startup sells no-tech tractors for half price
items.summary: Mechanical no-tech tractors aimed at cutting complexity and repair costs.
items.why_included: It taps the right-to-repair mood with a concrete physical product.
items.evidence: Commenters asked for a straightforward mechanical machine and noted these tractors could last for decades.

items.name: WSL9x
items.summary: A retro project that runs a modern Linux kernel inside Windows 9x.
items.why_included: It’s a rare, technical cross-OS hack with strong retro-computing appeal.
items.evidence: The thread compared it to Cygwin, CoLinux, flinux, WSL, Win4Lin, Interix, Wine, and Dosbox.

items.name: 5x5 Pixel font
items.summary: A tiny 5x5 bitmap font designed for cramped embedded screens.
items.why_included: It sparked practical recommendations for tiny-display typography.
items.evidence: Commenters debated glyph readability and linked multiple related micro-font projects.

items.name: Spleen
items.summary: A compact 5x8 font commenters recommended for tiny readable text.
items.why_included: It was explicitly praised as a practical alternative in the tiny-font discussion.
items.evidence: One reply said, 'I'm quite fond of Spleen,' while comparing small-font options.

items.name: Tom Thumb
items.summary: A tiny 4x6 font suggested as a readable alternative for micro displays.
items.why_included: It offers another concrete option for extremely constrained screens.
items.evidence: The thread cited Tom Thumb as a 4x6 font with all ASCII coverage and one fewer pixel per character.

items.name: Tony Pai's 3x3 pixel font
items.summary: A minimalist 3x3 uppercase pixel font for extremely small screens.
items.why_included: It broadens the set of workable fonts for ultra-small UIs.
items.evidence: A commenter linked Tony Pai's 3x3 font as a good uppercase-only option.

items.name: u8g2 fonts
items.summary: A font collection for embedded displays and other tiny UI surfaces.
items.why_included: It’s a practical source pack for small-screen and embedded projects.
items.evidence: Comments pointed to u8g2 as a useful embedded-display font library.
